topic: Taxation (Annual Rates for 2025–26, Compliance Simplification, and Remedial Measures) Bill
description: Wednesday 12 November 2025 | Finance and Expenditure Committee Taxation (Annual Rates for 2025–26, Compliance Simplification, and Remedial Measures) Bill Oceana Gold (New Zealand) Limited Paul Whelan, Commercial Manager, Oceana Gold Rachael McKinney, Superintendent – Financial Accounting, Oceana Gold Phil Stevenson, Partner, Deloitte Baucher Consulting Terry Baucher, Director Atmabhav Charitable Trust Amal Karl, Founding Trustee, Atmabhav Charitable Trust and Founder, CEO of FxMed Group Venkat Raman, Founding Trustee, Atmabhav Charitable Trust and Editor, General Manager of IndianNewsLink PwC Sandy Lau, Partner Chris Place, Partner St Peter's on Willis Social Justice Group Gail Duncan
descriptionSummary: Taxation (Annual Rates for 2025–26, Compliance Simplification, and Remedial Measures) Bill. Oceana Gold (New Zealand) Limited. Paul Whelan, Commercial Manager, Oceana Gold

chunks.text: We've got Stevenson here. Um, Anna Gold, nice to see you, Phil. We're in your hands. You've got 10 minutes as an organization, so we look forward to it. No problem at all. So, uh, I'd like to thank the committee for hearing our submission today. Um, we've also got in the audience, uh, Rachel McKinney and Paul Wheel and from Oceana Gold, and, and I'm a, a partner at Deloitte representing them as their tax advisor.

So our submission deals with a gap in the application of the investment boost rules on mining infrastructure. So the type of infrastructure I'm talking about, roads, dams, tunnels, et cetera. And the reason why we are submitting verbally today is that it's a bit easier to sort of talk through the why. Uh, we consider there's a bit of a gap in the rules and verbally rather than in writing.

And I think I'll just start by setting the same. So as we all know, investment Boost provides an immediate 20% deduction on assets that are commissioned in New Zealand. Now, mines are a little bit different to some other industries in that we have two types of assets in a mine, we have our plant and equipment, which is depreciated under ordinary tax rules and eligible for investment boost.

So that's all very well understood. And we also have our mining assets and that's the, the infrastructure of the mine. So predominantly land improvements, the roads, dams, tunnels that I talked about earlier. Now those assets are capitalized and they're amortized over the life of the mine.

That, um, cost base of the mining assets comes from two places. So it comes from expenditure that is incurred on assets built after the decision has been made to enter into commercial production. Now that expenditure is capital expenditure goes straight to balance sheet.

It's called mining development expenditure, and it's currently eligible for investment boost. But what we also have in our mining assets is there is also expenditure on assets that are built before the decision is made to enter into commercial production. So that's during what's called the exploration phase of the mine.

Now, the expenditure on those assets during the exploration phase is initially deductible in full because there's uncertainty around whether they will end up being a long term asset or not. If the mine's not viable, then that deduction becomes permanent, but if the mine is viable, so in other words, the decision is made to box on with commercial production, then that deduction's reversed.

The expenditure is treated as as income and it is added to the cost base of the mine and amortized over the life of the mine in exactly the same way that that post, um, decision to enter commercial production development expenditure is amortized.

The difference though, uh, from an investment boost perspective is that while that expenditure is treated the same as development expenditure, it is not included in the definition of development expenditure and accordingly is not included as a new investment asset for the purposes of investment boost.

So it's not eligible for investment boost. Now, the important thing here is that there's actually no distinction between the types of assets that are built during the exploration phase and during the development phase. The only distinction is the time of the mines lifecycle that the expenditure hasn't occurred.

So just coming back to that road dams, tunnels example, so the claw back expenditure that forms part of the mining assets, that is money spent on building these long term assets. And the only reason why there's an a deduction immediately is because it may be that the mine's not viable, so there's no long-term benefit from it.

Uh, but the way that the rules work is they do recognize that if, um, miners box on and, and develop their mine, that those assets will be used over the life of the mine. And so accordingly it would be inappropriate for them to have got a deduction in full right from the start.

A another way to put it is that if we didn't have this specific provision that allowed deductibility, it would just be capital expenditure and when the asset was commissioned, it would be eligible for investment boost. It's just that there's a step in between that, um, allows a deduction first.

So sort of to, to sum it all up, you know, um, our, our submission just says that it's logical that investment boost should apply to assets that arise from clawback exploration expenditure in the year that it's clawed back. And that's for three reasons. First of all, the money is spent on an asset and that's important.

The second thing is that money spent on the same type of asset post commercialization would be eligible for investment boost. And the third reason is that because we are saying that investment boost would only be able to be claimed when the expenditure is called back, that's consistent with the policy intent of investment boost that you can only claim it when you commission the asset.

And that's the point that the, um, assets commissioned. So that sort of, I suppose, sets out the why, uh, behind our submission and, and thank you to the committee for hearing it. Well, thank you very much. Uh, um, Phil, um, Rachel McKinney, have you got anything to, to add at this point? No, I think Phil has summarized it.

Okay. No, that, that's, that's fine. We'll go to questions and you, there might be something there for you. We'll go to Deborah Russell. Um, thank you for that. Um, sorry, I'm not especially familiar with these provisions. So, um, if you've got a mine and you've got, um, expiration expenditure going on, sorry, not a mine, a prospect I suppose.

Yeah. If you go ahead and develop that mine, the expiration of expenditure becomes part of the capital cost of the mine and can be amortized, depreciated, um, what happens if you do not go ahead with the, if the expiration is fruitless and you decide not to pursue further activity, what happens to that expenditure at, At that point the year it is deductible in full even though, so what you have is if you have a a failed investment Yeah, it's a hundred percent deductible.

Yeah. If you have a, um, not a viable investment, that's probably a better way to put it. Sure. If you have a viable investment, then what happens is, is that the previous cost ends up being reversed. So you pay tax on the reversal of the exploration of the expenditure, and then you, um, then you amortize it over the life of the mine.

One of the, okay, um, we'll check with our officials. One of the things that, um, became apparent with investment boost is that, um, a dead weight loss associated with it with, um, commercial buildings. So there was, um, it could be claimed for and in fact other asset where the decision to purchase had been made, but the actual purchase hadn't taken place, so happened particularly with commercial buildings, that was a problem.

Yeah. Is this gonna be the case as well with this mine, some of this mining development, uh, expiration expenditures? Some of that would've been decisions that's already made. So if you get the investment boost for it, it would represent, you know, it's one of those things that happens, a dead weight loss there. Yeah, That's right. I think, I mean, one way of looking at it is to say that, um, part of the decision making is to we whether to go ahead with commercial mining, that's all done on a any present value basis.

So cashflow modeling and early income, early cashflow savings are advantageous to the decision to go ahead. So if the spend was eligible for investment boost, then what that would do that would improve the early stage cash flows of the project, which, depending on how viable the project was, so if it was extremely viable, it would make no difference.

And you're absolutely right, it would be a dead weight loss. If it was marginal, then it could be the difference between going ahead at that particular point in time or not. Yeah. Okay. I guess, um, it's a policy issue really rather than 'cause the, the technical fix is gonna be straightforward. Right. So we'll just have to have to ask, um, get officials to talk to officials about that and see what That's right. Yeah. Yeah.

Okay. Thank You. Okay, thank you Deborah. We're gonna go to Ryan Hamilton. Thanks, GIA. Um, just to clarify two quick things. What you're seeking here is, I guess a, a broadening of the current definition so that your industry's captured in, in this and the investment boost piece. Is that correct? Uh, no, the, the industry's already captured and, um, and any of the spend on assets that are incurred after entering into commercial production are eligible for investment boost.

Uh, but what we have within the cost base of those same assets is some expenditure that has occurred earlier in the mine life and so has previously been deductible and then become non-deductible. And at the moment that sits outside of the investment boost rules. Okay. Simply because of the way that the mining rules are constructed in the act and that it separates clawed back expenditure from future development expenditure.

Okay. And can you just help articulate for me what difference the investment boost piece gives you in terms of marginal percentage versus the current depreciation? Amor amortization schedule? A a lot of it depends on the life of the mine itself. So there, there's two ways that the cost can be spread. It can be spread on a year's basis or it can be spread on units of production.

So for simplicity, I'll use the year's basis. If you've got a, um, 10 year mine life, then you'd deduct a 10% a year and say you would get the first 20% up front. If you've got a 20 year mine life, you know, then obviously the upfront deduction has, um, has more benefit to the miner and that mine life is assessed on an annual basis, so the amortization can even flow depending on the life of the mine at any given point in time.

Thank you. Okay. Thank you. Uh, we'll leave, we'll leave it at that. Um, Phil and Rachel, thank you so much for submitting, uh, to the committee this afternoon and for your written submission. No problem at all. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank You. Thank you. Right. We're gonna go on to, um, Terry Voucher and, uh, Terry welcome.

You've, uh, you've also got 10 minutes, uh, on, on behalf of, uh, your consulting firm, so we look forward to it. Sure. Uh, ki Han, thank you very much for this opportunity to speak with you. Um, background is and consulting firm. We do a lot in the space of advising clients moving to and from New Zealand or New Zealand based clients who have overseas investments that are in within the foreign investment fund, or fifth regime and financial arrangements regime.

And you will have seen, and I sent through earlier a diagram showing the, uh, in foreign investment flow chart, and I wanted to, I sent that through to the committee. Uh, I can share that if that would help. Yes, it would be great. Uh, yes. Okay. I've just sent a request. Um, alright,

I think, just carry on, Terry. Yep. So yeah, I'm just bringing it up to share. Um, and it's on the left hand side here, you can see the diagram showing what are the tracks that have to be followed in terms of different sorts of investments. Now as you can see, it's a bit of a, it's almost like a bowl of spaghetti.

It's been spilled on, uh, on the floor. Um, and my point that I wish to drive out and wish the, uh, tension for the committee is that this is an area where people with relatively modest means get fall into the regime. And it's, it's where I think there's substantial non-compliance or under compliance in this area because of the complexity of the regime.

So to the extent that the foreign investment, uh, regimes, um, revenue account method has come in, I'm cautiously optimistic of that because it sort of records with people's understandings of how a tax happens on a transactional basis. The fifth regime, because it works on a deemed basis, causes a great deal of confusion.

At any one time, we are dealing with at least one or two, uh, voluntary disclosures to inland revenue of under declared income in this space. And so the fifth and the the fifth regime is one area. The other area which is even more problematic is the financial arrangements regime.

And, and I'm glad to see the committee addressing the build addresses, the issue of the thresholds. Um, over here, the, the point here in which the stress is, this is even more complex and it applies to many people get caught on foreign mortgages. They've come here, they've kept a property overseas, they've rented it out, and they find that the exchange rate movement on the mortgage gets taxed.

Now I've noted a couple of determinations, these determinations, uh, you can see this book in here and they're back to back, but the thing to note for the committee is one dates from, uh, 24th of October, 1990 and the other one from 3rd of June, 2004, and they refer to legislation which has now been subsumed in one case, the 1976 Act and the other one to the 2019 94 act.

This is an area where a lot of work needs to be done, in my view, to, uh, simplify the regime for ordinary people. And what I would like to recommend strongly support the proposal to move, uh, the thresholds up to, uh, as, as in the bill. But I'd like to propose that the section 50 EW 57 3, which act acts a as a cal a deferral calculation, IU calculate the difference between what would be payable under an accrual basis or, uh, for accounting and a cash basis must be tracked.

That is something that's very difficult for ordinary taxpayers to do, and the threshold has been too low for too long, and I would request that is removed for a threshold, um, in full for people with a taxpayers with an income of under $250,000. I would also ask, and tying into the question I made about non-compliance is I think these thresholds should be increased with effect from the start of the current tax year.

We are already doing that in relation to the foreign investment fund revenue account method. I don't see why we should not, um, do so for the, um, these thresholds. It's a compliance measure, I think a relatively simplification, and I think the loss of revenue would almost be unquantifiable, but improvement in compliance is not would be something, uh, of benefit on the revenue fifth revenue account method.

Uh, the feedback I've had from clients is, doesn't go far enough. And if I may, I would like to quote from one client. She came back to me, he said, um, he said the government should think bigger and longer. There's a vast wealth in the world and New Zealand is a haven. Those who amassed wealth obviously have relevant expertise to start and grow companies or highly, technically competent and are risk takers who know how to make savvy investments.

They want to invest long term. The foreign investment fundraising is a bar to that or a, a hindrance to that in the American context. The tax credits is, there's a, a highly technical issue about whether any tax paid in relation to, um, the fifth regime regime is creditable against any US tax payable.

Um, we are into very deep weeds here, I'm afraid. Apologies for this. This is what I call the, um, quantum physics of the tax system. It's highly detailed, highly complex, but frankly not at all suited to capture. It's captures too many people, um, who is technically it's beyond their technical scope or expectations in terms of compliance.

And I think if we are looking to attract people, we, it's not just this all golden visa people we're talking about people bringing over maybe, uh, 50, uh, so a couple of hundred thousand dollars of savings and this regime puts perhaps some in a compliance, which I think is unjustified and I'm happy to take questions now.

Look, um, I take your points with respect to that. Yes, yes. Yeah. And I was, I was a bit puzzled by that because, um, uh, officials have told us that the amount of money involved is not particularly significant, but from what you are saying it actually might be because people don't really understand what they're supposed to be doing. Yes. Uh, uh, don't have it in front of me, but someone did a very good, um, analysis of, uh, obtained some excellent data under the Official Information Act, and I will share it with the committee.

I'll email it through after this. And they showed that the number of taxpayers reporting fifth income was rising sharply over the last three years. That's partly because inland revenue has expanded the tax return to get more detailed reporting on the matter. And so I think the number of taxpayers who reported has nearly tripled in that time.

So there is more tax at stake and income at stake here than perhaps might have been led to belief. But as I said, we regularly are reporting, um, uh, on portfolios typically around, um, the 200 to $500,000 size, which are maybe a hundred thousand pounds or or less.

Okay. Um, so the other thing I've sort of just got here is the, the fifth regime. Um, my understanding is that the people who mostly get badly caught up by, uh, people with US citizenship who've got these dual requirements to report their income, um, are you, is in your experience, are other people getting caught badly by the rules as well from other nations?

Or is it just, is it a compliance issue for them or is there a natural tax cost? Um, for the Americans, it's the worst case scenario. They have to do dual reporting on two different tax systems, one of which is realization based. That is a capital gains system, and the fifth regime is not realized based mostly.

And so they have issues with the fact that they are then reporting here and paying higher tax relative to what they would pay in the United States, and they can't claim the credits on other jurisdictions who, uh, for other people, the, the issue is around compliance. They simply are not aware that these, uh, requirements exist because the portfolios, they're not making many changes to them.

People are rather, um, lazy is not the right word, but um, under manage share portfolios. So they sort of tend to set and forget, but they know if they make a sale they have to report it, but that's a transaction. Um, and it, many of the people I deal with come from CGT jurisdictions or transactional jurisdictions where something happens.

Yeah. We know we have to report something. The fifth regime and the financial arrange regime arrangements regime are completely counterintuitive to that in that they are not transactional. The, the liability to tax arises even if you have not sold in the portfolio. And that was at 39%, nearly $120,000 of tax the following year it moved back the other way. Thank you Cam. Sure. Uh, good morning, uh, members of the committee and, uh, thank you for the opportunity to speak with you today. I would like to take this opportunity to highlight some points from our written submission.

ABA Charitable Trust is a New Zealand registered charity dedicated to supporting communities living in extreme poverty, primarily in India. And over time we will support charities in New Zealand as well. We are here today to respectfully respect, uh, to request overseas Tony status under this bill.

Our application was lodged with IRD in September 24. We have since complied with the various directives provided by the policy advisor to meet the requirements. We have not sought any donations as yet, but our website and payment portal are set up and the trust will be launched at the upcoming Indian News Link Business Awards later this month.

So why does this matter? At its heart? This request is about fairness, compassion, and trust. Millions overseas still lack necessities such as food, clothing, clean water, and access to health facilities and education. See Doni status to creates a transparent, well-governed channel, which ensures that donors can receive the same tax benefits for their general generosity as they would when supporting a local charity or other overseas charities which have this doni status.

The next question is why India? Our initial focus is India because our trustees have deep local roots and longstanding relationships with trusted NGOs on the ground. Besides, as you well know, the impact per dollar donated is extraordinary In India as an ex, as an example, just one New Zealand dollar can provide two nutritious meals for an adult for a day.

Small contributions truly transform lives. You will be partnering with well-established charities, which have an excellent track record of good governance practices. Medical foundation is the first charity, which was founded 25 years ago by an American philanthropist, which provides family-based care for orphaned and abandoned children.

Apai will most likely be the second charity, which was founded more than 50 years ago by Tom Holland, the then Australian Council Council General in India, which empowers families in urban slums. The most important aspect for any charity is obviously governance and oversight.

We have built a trust with strong governance and zero administrative costs with help of volunteers. So donations can go directly to the projects. Our trustees include respected leaders from law, business and media, people of integrity who provide independent oversight and accountability. All of the five trustees have lived in New Zealand for some 30 to 40 years and have contributed to New Zealand charity initiatives.

We have reviewed audited financial statements from partners will meet regularly with them and conduct site visits to verify that funds reach the intended beneficiaries. Reporting cycles from our partners will be on a quarterly or six monthly basis depending on the project being funded. Funding is project specific with clear outcomes agreed between the two parties.

Transparency is a principle we live by and expect the same from the NGOs we will work with. All agreements with the recipients will have a termination clause, which can be exercised by our trust. The heart of al, the word al in Sanskrit means to feel another suffering as your own.

That's what drives us. Our guiding belief is simple. Our life is life, everyone matters. We see giving not as a charity, but as a shared human duty, one that uplifts both the giver and the receiver. In closing members, we respectfully ask the to grant overseas Tony status to charitable trust, recognize a strong governance and transparent model, and enable New Zealanders to extend their compassion globally through a safe and trusted charitable pathway.

With your support, we can turn compassion into meaningful action. If I may now just end on a small personal note. I came to New Zealand from India nearly 40 years ago, but I have not forgotten where I came from. The faces of people who still dream are pet alive, aha is my way and our trustees' way of giving back, of connecting the kindness of New Zealanders with those who need it most.

And I'm just based down the road here in Wellington. Um, I am joined by Chris Place. Um, given the limited time that we have with you today, we would like to make some brief comments, um, on the proposed RAM method and FBT changes before, um, discussing our submissions on the ESS proposals in a bit more detail. However, we do consider more needs would need to be done to, um, really increase New Zealand's um, attractiveness to migrants. Um, the main example, at the main example being the tax rate that's applied, um, to the income that will rise on the disposal of any eligible RAM interests. So when you take that into account, um, this in and in contrast to other jurisdictions that we compare to ourselves to, for example, Australia, they would tax that gain at 23.5% or in the us um, where the top federal tax rate is 23.8. So in some cases under the proposed RAM method, um, and the individuals will most likely still pay more tax as a result of moving to New Zealand, um, notwithstanding that 30% discount.

Um, in addition, we have outlined in our written submissions a number of changes that could be made in our view that, um, that would make the proposed if it, uh, method more, um, effective. Um, it would be great to see that work progress with more substantive, um, changes being made to the FBT regime. Um, now on the three key points I'd like to touch on are, um, the deferral regime, which is proposed, um, the unconditional right to presently receive shares, which is a, a change in the taxing point and, and the employer deduction. Now on the deferral regime, um, this is, you know, from, from our perspective and from the perspective of our clients, this is an underwhelming, um, proposition.

Um, our clients, particularly in the, in the venture capital and startup community, have been asking for a concessionary regime, which enables them to compete with our major trading partners, all of whom have concessionary ESS regimes. This proposal will not provide any concessions, will not level the playing field, and, um, does not do anything which cannot already be achieved under the existing legislation.

Our VC and and startup community is very disadvantaged by virtue of geography already by access to capital by distance. This is not an area we should be disadvantaged in. We can really help them. This is something we already can do. Now we know that officials who were considering these proposals had some really tight parameters placed around them that there were no concessions on the table to be considered.

And we would, we propose, we submit that that is something which needs to be looked at in order for our, um, New Zealand Inc. To be competitive on a global stage. And we, and it is not hard to achieve and we are very willing to work with officials in order to assist them as we did with the 2018 legislation.
